original_abstract:
This research study explores the perspectives of registered nurses regarding patient safety and the application of restrictive measures within hospital settings in the Czech Republic. Conducted as a sociological survey using structured interviews, the study analyzed a representative sample of 772 nurses working shifts in inpatient departments across various regions and medical specialties. 

The findings indicate that a significant majority of nursing staff perceive the risk of patient injury during restraint (specifically physical strapping) as either negligible (48%) or low (36.9%). However, nurses stationed in surgical and internal medicine departments reported a higher perceived risk of injury during bed-based restriction compared to their colleagues in other specialties. Regarding procedural compliance, 91.3% of respondents confirmed the presence of institutional standards or internal regulations governing the use of restraints for aggressive, restless, or self-harming patients. Furthermore, 65.5% of the nurses surveyed reported that they perform immobilization and restraint based on a physician’s order in justified clinical cases.

The study also identified significant correlations between general safety concerns and the use of restraints; nurses who viewed their wards as less safe prone to higher estimates of injury during restrictive procedures. The strongest association was found between the perceived probability of suicide attempts and the likelihood of health damage during isolation. To mitigate the clinical and ethical risks associated with these procedures, the authors emphasize the necessity of continuous staff education, regular nursing care audits to verify standard compliance, and the strict limitation of restrictive measures to cases where they are absolutely essential and professionally justified.
humanized_abstract:
Title of thesis: Attitudes of registered nurses towards patient safety and the use of restraint in hospitals in the Czech Republic Abstract: This thesis deals with the attitudes of registered nurses towards patient safety and the use of restraint in hospitals in the Czech Republic. The research was carried out as a sociological survey that consisted of structured interviews. Data were collected from a representative sample of 772 nurses employed on night and afternoon shifts in wards of selected hospitals in different regions of the Czech Republic and in several medical branches.

The results of the study show that the majority of the nursing staff assessed the probability of injury to the patient during the application of physical coercion (physical restraint) as small (48%) or moderate (36.9%). The assessment of risk of injury to the patient during the bed restraint in surgical and internal medicine departments was higher than in other departments. In relation to the procedural compliance, 91.3% of the nurses confirmed that in their institution there were regulations and rules for the application of physical coercion on aggressive, restless or self-destructive patients. In addition, 65.5% of the nurses stated that they immobilized and restrained the patients according to the physician’s decision in cases where it was medically justified.

The study also found correlations between general safety concerns and the use of restraints: Nurses who believed their wards to be less safe estimated a higher likelihood of injuries during restraint. The most pronounced correlation was found between estimated probability of suicide attempts and the risk of incurring health damage during isolation. The authors therefore call for the implementation of training for medical staff on an ongoing basis, audits of nursing care to verify compliance with guidelines, and strict restrictions on the use of physical or pharmaceutical restraints, except in cases where they are deemed medically and professionally necessary.
